title: The Out-of-Vocabulary Limit
concept: Word tokenizers fail on new, misspelled, or rare words; subword tokenization solves this by breaking words into smaller, reusable pieces.

exercises.prompt: What is the term for a word that a tokenizer has never seen before in its training data?
exercises.explain: When a tokenizer encounters a word it has never seen before, it labels it as Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV), which often causes errors or loss of meaning.

cards.title: The Mystery Box Problem
cards.body: What happens when an AI reads a word that wasn't in its dictionary, like a new slang word or a typo? If it uses a simple word-by-word tokenizer, it gets completely confused and treats the word as a blank mystery box. Let's learn how AIs avoid this trap!

cards.title: What is Subword Tokenization?
cards.body: When an AI model meets a word it has never seen before, we call it an Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) word. Word tokenizers just give up on these. Subword tokenizers solve this by splitting unknown words into smaller pieces (like prefixes, roots, and suffixes) that it does know.

cards.title: The Lego Box Solution
cards.body: Imagine you only have whole-word stickers to write messages. If you want to write 'hangry' but don't have that exact sticker, you are stuck! A subword tokenizer is like a box of Lego bricks: instead of whole words, you have syllables and letters. You can easily build the new word 'hangry' by snapping together 'han' and 'gry'!

cards.title: Breaking it Down
cards.body:
Imagine the AI has never seen the word 'unhelpful'. 

A traditional word tokenizer sees:
• 'unhelpful' -> ❌ Unknown! (OOV)

A subword tokenizer sees:
• 'un' + 'help' + 'ful' -> 3 known pieces! -> ⚠️ Solved!

cards.title: The Giant Dictionary Myth
cards.body: Some people think subword tokenizers have to store every single word in the dictionary to work. In fact, they do the opposite! By storing just a few thousand common word parts (like 'ing', 'pre', 'est'), they can build and understand millions of different words!

cards.title: Word vs. Subword
cards.body: Compare how the two systems handle the brand new slang word: 'hyperactive'.

exercises.prompt: What is the main limitation of a basic word tokenizer?

options.text: It cannot understand words that were not in its training data.
It tokenizes words too quickly for the computer.
It automatically translates slang into other languages.
It limits the number of sentences you can type.

exercises.explain: The Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) limit happens because a basic word tokenizer can only recognize words it was specifically trained on. New words are treated as completely unknown.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a user makes a tiny typo, a subword tokenizer will fail and treat the whole sentence as unknown.
exercises.explain: Subword tokenizers are highly effective at handling typos because they can break the misspelled word down to isolate the typo, rather than failing on the entire word.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the sentence:
exercises.sentence: To handle brand new words without errors, modern AI models use a _______ tokenizer.
exercises.explain: Subword tokenizers break down unknown or rare words into smaller, familiar pieces so the AI can still understand them.

exercises.prompt: An AI uses a subword tokenizer and encounters the rare word 'sporty'. Which output shows how it would process this word?

options.text: s | p | o | r | t | y

exercises.explain: The prompt asks for a subword tokenization output. Option B correctly shows the word 'sporty' broken into the prefix/root 'sport' and the suffix 'y'. Option A is character-level, and Option C is word-level.

exercises.prompt: Your friend is building a chat app but keeps getting errors when users type new slang like 'unfriend'. Why would switching from a word tokenizer to a subword tokenizer fix this?

options.text: A word tokenizer can easily guess the meaning of brand new words by looking at their letters.
A word tokenizer will completely fail or ignore 'unfriend', while a subword tokenizer can break it into 'un' and 'friend'.
A word tokenizer is much better at handling spelling mistakes than a subword tokenizer.
A subword tokenizer ignores new words to save computer memory space.

exercises.explain: Word tokenizers treat whole words as single units and cannot process unseen words. Subword tokenizers break unfamiliar words down into smaller, known units (like 'play' + 'ful' + 'ly'), preventing vocabulary errors.
